items.q: Should a project be accepted just because its payback period is short?
items.correct: Not necessarily — payback ignores cash flows after the cutoff and the time value of money; NPV and average rate of return give a fuller picture of worth.
items.misconception: The investment with the fastest payback is always the best.
items.random_wrong: Payback period measures how long customers take to pay invoices.
items.why: HL students must critique investment appraisal methods, not just compute them.

items.q: Does a high gearing ratio mean a company is failing?
items.correct: No — high gearing means heavy reliance on borrowed funds, which raises risk but can boost returns to shareholders when profits exceed interest costs.
items.misconception: Debt on the balance sheet is always a sign of trouble.
items.random_wrong: Gearing measures the number of machines a firm owns.
items.why: Students moralise debt; gearing reflects a risk-return choice, not a verdict on the firm.

items.q: Is working capital the same as cash?
items.correct: No — working capital is current assets minus current liabilities, including stock and receivables; a firm can have positive working capital yet little cash.
items.misconception: Working capital is the money in the till and the bank.
items.random_wrong: Working capital is the annual wages budget.
items.why: Understanding the composition of working capital lets students diagnose liquidity problems properly.

items.q: Luca Pacioli's 'Summa de Arithmetica' detailing double-entry bookkeeping dates from:
items.why: Correct answer: 1494. “1390” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Net present value. “Payback” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Year Pacioli published 'Summa' codifying double-entry bookkeeping?
items.why: The real figure is 1494 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
